name: Estonia
summary: A strong e-Estonia tech scene, flat 22% tax, an affordable English-taught CS MSc and a now-shorter 3-of-5-year A2 PR route make Estonia a credible study->work pathway for two engineers; the biggest levers are easy graduate work-switching and low tax, while the decisive drags are the no-dual-citizenship rule (renounce Bangladeshi nationality), an 8-year + B1-Estonian citizenship path, a New Delhi trip for the D-visa, and a tiny ~1.3M market for two senior engineers at once.

links.title: Estonian Police and Border Guard Board - migration (politsei.ee)

job-market.summary: ICT is a top OSKA shortage (~1,550 ICT shortfall/yr); avg Tallinn SW pay ~EUR 48,700, but a ~1.3M market limits depth for two seniors.
job-market.reasoning: Estonia has a globally outsized startup/tech economy (Bolt, Wise, Skype heritage) and OSKA projects an annual shortfall of ~1,550 ICT specialists to 2035, with software developers, analysts and AI/cyber roles in highest demand; many ICT roles sit outside the immigration quota and the May 2026 Aliens Act cut the top-specialist wage bar from 2x to 1.5x average salary. English is the working language in most tech firms. The decisive caveat for this couple is scale: at ~1.3M people the depth of senior roles for two engineers at once is thinner than larger hubs, and gross salaries (~EUR 48,700 avg, to ~EUR 67k total comp) trail Western Europe.
evidence: OSKA: ~1,550 ICT specialist shortfall per year to 2035; software devs/analysts most needed
Average Tallinn software engineer ~EUR 48,700 gross; range to ~EUR 67k total comp (2026)
May 2026 Aliens Act: top-specialist wage bar cut from 2x to 1.5x average salary
Many ICT roles exempt from the annual immigration quota; English-first workplaces
Skilled-worker exemption ~1,300 permits/yr, rising to 2,600 if GDP grows >=2%
Small market: ~1.3M population caps simultaneous senior-role depth for two engineers

links.title: Working in Estonia (Study in Estonia, official)

visa-access.summary: No Estonian mission in Dhaka; BD students must travel to Estonian Embassy New Delhi for the D-visa, prove EUR 800/mo, give biometrics in person.
visa-access.reasoning: Estonia has no embassy in Bangladesh, and long-stay D-visas cannot be lodged at the German (Schengen-representing) embassy in Dhaka, so Bangladeshi students must apply in person at the Estonian Embassy in New Delhi, with 10-fingerprint biometrics and proof of ~EUR 800/month in funds. The New Delhi mission decides D-visa cases in 7-15 working days and there is no BD-specific ban, but the cross-border travel, in-person appointment and lack of any Dhaka processing are real friction versus countries that decide in Dhaka. Bilateral ties are modest but cordial (~500 BD students already in Estonia), keeping this in the mixed band rather than lower.
evidence: No Estonian embassy in Bangladesh; D-visa cannot be lodged in Dhaka
Bangladeshi students must apply in person at Estonian Embassy New Delhi
Financial proof for students: EUR 800/month required (New Delhi mission, official)
In-person submission with 10-fingerprint biometrics; appointment-only booking
D-visa decision 7-15 working days (New Delhi mission)
~500 Bangladeshi students already enrolled in Estonia; ties modest but cordial

links.title: Documents required for a long-stay D-visa (Estonian Embassy New Delhi, official)
For Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka and other third-country citizens (Estonian Embassy New Delhi, official)
Long-stay visa for students (Estonian Embassy New Delhi, official)

citizenship.summary: 8 years residence (last 5 permanent) + B1 Estonian + Constitution exam, and NO dual citizenship - must renounce Bangladeshi nationality.
citizenship.reasoning: Naturalisation requires at least 8 years of residence with the final 5 on a permanent basis, plus passing the Estonian B1 language exam and an exam on the Constitution and Citizenship Act, with stable income and an oath. Critically, Estonia does not permit dual citizenship for naturalised adults, so this couple would have to renounce Bangladeshi nationality - directly conflicting with their stated dual-citizenship preference. The long timeline, hard Finno-Ugric language bar and renunciation requirement place this near the bottom band for them, despite a strong passport (visa-free EU/Schengen).
evidence: 8 years residence required, last 5 on a permanent basis (politsei.ee, 2026)
B1 Estonian language exam + Constitution/Citizenship Act exam required
Dual citizenship not allowed for naturalised adults; must renounce prior nationality
Renunciation directly conflicts with the couple's stated dual-citizenship preference
Permanent-basis clock effectively starts only after long-term residence status
Upside: Estonian passport is strong (EU/Schengen, broad visa-free access)

links.title: Conditions - Estonian citizenship for an adult (politsei.ee, official)

post-study-work.summary: 270-day (~9-month) job-search extension after graduation; graduates switch to a work permit with no UIF approval or strict wage gate.
post-study-work.reasoning: Graduates of Estonian universities may extend their stay up to 270 days (~9 months) to seek qualified employment, applying to the Police and Border Guard Board before the study permit expires. On signing a contract they switch in-country to a residence permit for employment under special graduate conditions - employers do not need Unemployment Insurance Fund approval and are exempt from strict salary requirements - which is genuinely easy. As two graduates, each partner holds their own extension and work permit, so both work; the main limit is that 9 months is shorter than the 18-36 month windows in Germany, Australia or Canada.
evidence: 270-day (~9-month) job-search extension after graduation (PBGB)
Apply to PBGB before the study residence permit expires; processing ~30-60 days
Graduate work permit: no UIF approval and exempt from strict salary requirements
In-country switch to employment permit (initially 2 years, renewable)
Both graduate partners can each hold their own extension and work permit
9-month window shorter than 18-36 month PSW in Germany/Australia/Canada

links.title: Working / after studies (Study in Estonia, official)

spouse-family.summary: Both are engineers, so each holds their own study/work permit and works freely; a family-member residence permit also grants unlimited work rights.
spouse-family.reasoning: For this couple the cleanest path is for BOTH to enrol then work on their own permits, because settling as the family member of someone who is themselves only a student is allowed only in exceptional cases. As two CS graduates each can hold an independent study then employment permit, so both work in every phase. Usefully, once a holder of a temporary residence permit (e.g. an employed engineer) sponsors a spouse, the family member's residence permit carries an unlimited right to work, study or do business - so the dependant track is workable too. The minor friction is that a study-stage dependant route is restricted and dependant work is not automatic at the student phase.
evidence: Family members with temporary right of residence have unlimited right to work/study/business (politsei.ee)
Settling as family member of a student only allowed in exceptional cases
Both partners can instead each hold their own study then work permit
Couple can qualify individually OR via the family-member route once one works
Children of residence-permit holders access schooling; family reunification available
Dependant work rights not automatic during the student phase

links.title: Family members temporary right of residence (politsei.ee, official)
EU immigration portal - family member, Estonia (official)

msc-study.summary: English-taught CS MSc at Tartu (~EUR 7,200/yr) and TalTech IT (~EUR 3,000-6,000/yr); no student work-hour cap; cheap vs UK/Australia.
msc-study.reasoning: Both leading universities run English-taught Computer Science master's: University of Tartu charges EUR 7,200/year for non-EU students with merit waivers (up to 50% of students get a waiver, 10 slots open to all citizenships), and TalTech IT/CS programmes run roughly EUR 3,000-6,000/year. Tuition is affordable versus the UK/Australia, and crucially Estonia sets no hourly cap on work for enrolled full-time students, so both can earn while studying and a spouse can accompany. The main negative is that Tartu is ending most non-EU tuition waivers for students admitted from 2026/27, raising the effective cost for newcomers.
evidence: University of Tartu CS MSc: EUR 7,200/year (non-EU), English-taught (official)
Tartu waivers: up to 50% of students; 10 full waivers open to all citizenships
TalTech IT/CS master's roughly EUR 3,000-6,000/year
No hourly limit on work for enrolled full-time students in Estonia
Spouse may accompany; for this couple both enrolling is the cleaner route
Tartu phasing out most non-EU tuition waivers for students admitted from 2026/27

links.title: Computer Science MSc (University of Tartu, official)

pr-pathway.summary: From 2026, long-term/PR needs only 3 of 5 years residence + adaptation programme + A2 Estonian (not B1) - a shorter, clearer route than headline 5y.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Estonia's temporary residence permit for permanent settlement (long-term residence) requires having resided in Estonia at least three years over five consecutive years, with sufficient income and health insurance - a relatively quick, predictable route. From 1 January 2026 applicants must also complete the adaptation programme (basic module) and reach Estonian at A2 level (not B1), which adds an integration step but a lower language bar than citizenship. For this couple the three-of-five-year horizon plus achievable A2 is workable; the friction is the new adaptation/language requirement and that the tighter integration rules signal future policy can move.
evidence: Long-term/PR permit: resided in Estonia at least 3 years over 5 consecutive years (Integration Foundation, official)
From 1 Jan 2026: adaptation programme (basic module) + Estonian at A2 required
A2 language bar is lower than the B1 needed for citizenship
Needs stable legal income and health insurance
Applications filed before 1 Jan 2026 processed under prior rules
Tightened integration criteria signal some policy-direction risk

links.title: Requirements: adaptation programme + A2 for permanent residence (Integration Foundation, official)

income-cost.summary: SW pay ~EUR 48,700-67k in Tallinn vs ~EUR 600 city 1-bed rent; flat 22% tax + ~1.6% employee contribution leaves a strong net on two incomes.
income-cost.reasoning: Average Tallinn software-engineer pay is ~EUR 48,700 gross, with ranges to ~EUR 67,000 total comp and top firms (Bolt ~EUR 84k); the flat 22% income tax plus only ~1.6% employee unemployment contribution leaves high take-home. Tallinn rent is moderate (~EUR 600 for a city-centre 1-bed, ~EUR 1,594/month total single-person living per Numbeo), so two engineering incomes save well. Gross pay trails Western Europe, which caps the score, but cost-adjusted savings on two incomes are solid.
evidence: Average Tallinn SW engineer ~EUR 48,700 gross; range to ~EUR 67k total comp (2026)
Top employer Bolt ~EUR 84k average total comp
Tallinn 1-bed city-centre rent ~EUR 600/mo; ~EUR 1,594/mo single-person living (Numbeo/Wise 2026)
Flat 22% income tax + only ~1.6% employee unemployment contribution
EUR 700/month tax-free allowance from 2026 lifts net pay
Two incomes + low employee deductions enable strong savings

healthcare.summary: Universal EHIF for employed residents via 13% social-tax health portion; highly digitised; small specialist visit fees up to EUR 20.
healthcare.reasoning: The Estonian Health Insurance Fund (Tervisekassa/EHIF) covers all legally employed residents, funded by the health portion (13%) of the employer's social tax, and the system is among Europe's most digitised (e-prescriptions, national e-health records). GP visits are free; specialist self-liability fees are capped at up to EUR 20. Coverage activates after the first social-tax payment, so newcomers and self-funded students should hold private cover (also required for the visa) during the gap - a minor onboarding friction rather than a structural weakness, hence a strong but not top score.
evidence: EHIF covers legally employed residents via the 13% health portion of employer social tax
GP visits free; specialist self-liability fee capped at up to EUR 20 (from Apr 2025)
Highly digitised: e-prescriptions and national e-health portal
Coverage activates after first social-tax payment; gap covered by private/visa insurance
Students must hold travel/medical insurance for the D-visa anyway
Under-19s get free dental care from contracted dentists

links.title: Visit and in-patient fees from 1 April 2025 (Tervisekassa, official)

culture-language.summary: English widely used in tech and cities, leading e-government, but Estonian (A2 for PR, B1 for citizenship) is a hard Finno-Ugric officialdom bar.
culture-language.reasoning: English is broadly spoken, especially among younger people and in the English-first tech sector, and digital-first e-government services are easy to use, giving good day-to-day livability. The constraint for this couple's settlement goal is that Estonian is required for officialdom - A2 for long-term residence and B1 for citizenship - and Estonian (a non-Indo-European Finno-Ugric language) is notably hard. Climate is cold and dark in winter and the society is small and homogeneous, so newcomers stand out; daily life and work run fine in English, but the local-language requirement and limited diversity hold this in the decent-but-friction band.
evidence: English widely spoken, especially in tech and among youth
Leading e-government; most services usable digitally in English
Estonian at A2 required for PR (from 2026); B1 for citizenship
Estonian is Finno-Ugric and notably hard for newcomers
Cold, dark winters; small, homogeneous society (newcomers visible)
Integration easier in Tallinn/Tartu international tech circles

links.title: Living in Estonia (Study in Estonia, official)

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law 2025: score 0.82, among global leaders (~top 10-11 of 143); low crime, strong institutions, fair courts.
safety-law.reasoning: Estonia scores 0.82 in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (covering 143 countries), ranking among global leaders alongside the Nordics, Germany and Ireland, and was one of the few states to improve year-on-year. Crime is low, courts are fair, and corruption is among the lowest in the region. This is a top-tier, orderly and safe environment for skilled migrants; the only minor caveat is a small visible-minority population, so newcomers are more conspicuous than in larger, more diverse hubs.
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: overall score 0.82, among global leaders (143 countries)
Improved modestly year-on-year; cited with Denmark/Finland/Sweden/Germany/Ireland
Low crime; strong, fair judicial institutions
Low corruption relative to regional peers
Small minority population; newcomers more visible

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index - Estonia

politics.summary: Stable EU/NATO democracy easing skilled-worker rules (May 2026 Aliens Act), but quota tightened to 1,292, far-right EKRE ~16%, and Russia-border tension.
politics.reasoning: Estonia is a stable EU/NATO democracy whose May 2026 Aliens Act amendments eased skilled migration (change employer without a new permit, longer unemployment grace, lower top-specialist wage bar). Tempering this: the non-EU immigration quota was tightened to 1,292 for 2026 and integration rules for settlement got stricter; the far-right EKRE polls ~16% opposing large-scale immigration; and the coalition (Michal's Reform/Estonia 200, after expelling the SDE in March 2025) faces volatility before 2027. The Russia border drives 5%-of-GDP defence spending and occasional incidents (e.g. a March 2026 drone strike near Narva), a real but managed geopolitical risk.
evidence: May 2026 Aliens Act: change employer without new permit, longer unemployment grace, lower wage bar
Non-EU immigration quota tightened to 1,292 for 2026; stricter settlement criteria from Jan 2026
Stable EU/NATO member; Michal's Reform/Estonia 200 coalition (SDE expelled Mar 2025)
Far-right EKRE ~16% vote share, opposes large-scale immigration
Defence spending raised to 5% of GDP from 2026 amid Russia-border tension
Foreign intelligence: no expected Russian attack near-term, but readiness raised for ~2027

tax.summary: Flat 22% income tax, universal EUR 700/mo allowance, only ~1.6% employee contribution; very high take-home for two engineers.
tax.reasoning: Estonia levies a simple flat 22% personal income tax (a planned rise to 24% was cancelled by Parliament in 2025), with a universal EUR 700/month (EUR 8,400/year) tax-free allowance from 2026 after the 'tax hump' was abolished, so the allowance no longer phases out for high earners. Employees pay only ~1.6% unemployment insurance (the 33% social tax is employer-side, plus an optional 2-6% pension), giving a total employee burden of ~23.6% and very high take-home - highly attractive for two software salaries and one of the simplest systems in the EU.
evidence: Flat 22% personal income tax; planned 24% rise cancelled (2025)
Universal EUR 700/month (EUR 8,400/year) tax-free allowance from 2026
'Tax hump' abolished - allowance no longer phases out for high earners
Employee pays only ~1.6% unemployment insurance (+ optional 2-6% pension)
Total employee burden ~23.6%; 33% social tax is employer-side
EUR 50k salary: 22% applies only on EUR 41,600 after allowance

links.title: Estonia tax changes 2026: income tax, payroll, social tax (1Office)

muslim-diaspora.summary: Tiny Muslim community (~10,000, 0.73%), mainly Tatar/Azeri; small ~500 Bangladeshi diaspora (mostly students); limited halal/mosque access in Tallinn.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Estonia's Muslim population is very small - ~10,000 (0.73%) by 2024 estimate, up from ~4,000 in 2008 - dominated by Tatars and Azeris rather than South Asians. The Bangladeshi community numbers only ~500, largely IT/business students, so there is a thin but real network. Religious infrastructure is sparse: worship concentrates at centres in Tallinn (Estonian Islamic Centre / Turath Islamic Cultural Centre, which also sells halal meat), with limited halal dining especially outside Tallinn. Society is secular and broadly tolerant, but the near-absence of a sizeable Bangladeshi/South-Asian community and thin halal/mosque access are major drawbacks for this couple.
evidence: ~10,000 Muslims (0.73%) estimated 2024, up from ~4,000 in 2008
Community mainly Tatar and Azeri, not South Asian
~500 Bangladeshis in Estonia, mostly IT/business students
Worship concentrated at Tallinn centres; halal meat sold at Turath Islamic Cultural Centre
Limited halal dining, especially outside Tallinn; little halal labelling
Secular, broadly tolerant society; no purpose-built mosque
